1: ʾēlêkā nāśāʾtî ʾet-ʿênay hayyōšĕbî baššāmāyim — "to you I lift my eyes, O you who sit enthroned in the heavens!" — the opening gesture (lifting eyes) echoes Psalms 121:1 but with a crucial difference: here the eyes are lifted directly to YHWH, not ambiguously toward the hills. hayyōšĕbî baššāmāyim (the one who sits/is enthroned in the heavens) — the divine title stresses sovereign rule from above; this is the same God who bent down to look in Psalms 113:6. The pilgrim looks up to find the one who has already looked down.
2: hinnēh kĕʿênê ʿăbādîm ʾel-yad ʾădônêhem kĕʿênê šipḥāh ʾel-yad gĕbirtāh kēn ʿênênû ʾel-yhwh ʾĕlōhênû ʿad šeyĕḥānnēnû — the sustained analogy of master/servant and mistress/maidservant. The servant watches the master's hand (yad) — in the ancient household economy, a subtle gesture of the master's hand gave the servant instructions without words. The servant's whole attention is on the hand that may signal need or command. ʿad šeyĕḥānnēnû (until he has mercy on us) — the waiting is not passive but attentive, watchful. The plural ʿênênû (our eyes) shifts to the whole congregation.
3: ḥānnēnû yhwh ḥānnēnû kî-rab śābaʿnû bûz — "have mercy on us, O LORD, have mercy on us — for we have endured far more than our fill of contempt" — the doubled ḥānnēnû (have mercy on us / be gracious to us) is urgent petition. rab śābaʿnû bûz (we are exceedingly filled with contempt) — bûz (contempt, scorn, despising) is a cutting social wound, worse than hostility: to be despised is to be treated as of no account. śābaʿnû (we are filled/sated) — as one becomes surfeited with food, the psalmist is surfeited with contempt; they have had more than enough.
4: rabbat śābeʿāh lāh napšēnû hallaʿag haššaʾănannîm habbûz ligĕʾônîm — "we have had more than our fill of the mockery of the comfortable and the contempt of the arrogant" — laʿag (mockery, derision, scorn) from lāʿag, to mock, stammer at. šaʾănannîm (those at ease, the comfortable, the prosperous) — the word ironically echoes šaʾănān (ease, security); those who are comfortable mock the pilgrim community. gĕʾônîm (the proud, arrogant, from gāʾah, to be high, exalted) — pride and ease together: the psalm identifies social contempt as coming from those whose comfort makes them forget their own contingency.
